idea.hook: Space itself can be a GIANT lens!
facts: Massive galaxy clusters have such strong gravity they warp the fabric of spacetime around them.
This warped spacetime acts like a cosmic magnifying glass, bending and focusing light from distant objects behind the cluster.
Astronomers observe distorted 'arcs' or multiple images of single distant galaxies, some magnified by over 100x, thanks to this effect.
idea.payoff: We literally see galaxies that would otherwise be invisible, thanks to gravity's cosmic magic trick!
idea.title: Gravity's Cosmic Magnifying Glass! 🔭🌌

scenes.text: Space itself can be a GIANT lens!
scenes.visual: Dark cosmic background. A large, distant galaxy cluster appears, subtly distorting the background light around it, hinting at a magnifying effect.
Zoom into the galaxy cluster. A subtle grid overlay appears, showing the fabric of spacetime bending and curving around the cluster's mass.

scenes.text: This acts like a cosmic magnifying glass.
scenes.visual: Light rays from a tiny, distant galaxy (behind the cluster) are shown bending around the warped spacetime of the cluster and converging towards the viewer's perspective.
A stunning, authentic or hyper-realistic simulated image of a galaxy cluster (e.g., from Hubble) prominently featuring elongated lensed 'arcs' and multiple images of background galaxies.

scenes.text: See invisible galaxies thanks to gravity's cosmic magic!
scenes.visual: Transition to a wider, awe-inspiring view of the cosmos, filled with numerous distant, lensed galaxies, emphasizing the scale of discovery and wonder. Stars twinkle subtly.
